text: Higher education in Africa is undergoing fundamental change. Enrollments are increasing dramatically, rapidly and by a huge margin across the African continent, resulting in what can be described as a 'flash flood' of students. There is increased pressure on the universities’ finances, governance, quality of education and also graduate employment. Meanwhile, higher education in Africa remains the most underdeveloped system globally, at a time when the system is confronted with increasing access to higher education while at the same time maintaining quality and relevance. This introductory article welcomes the launch of the International Journal of African Higher Education and it provides a snapshot of the developments and transformations currently affecting higher education in Africa. It situates some of the core issues highlighted in the papers that are published in this inaugural edition of the Journal, covering topics such as the financing of higher education; gender equity; internationalisation of higher education and the drive for graduate studies in Africa.
explanation: The text reads like a polished, domain-specific academic introduction with coherent structure and terminology, typical of human authorship; while AI could produce similar prose, there are no obvious AI-generated markers here.
